citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
There were three domestic pigeons (2-Blue bar, 1-Blue/White) that had overgrown upper beaks that protruded downward
past the lower beak. The overgrown beaks had not been identified by facility representatives.
Overgrown beaks can impact a bird's ability to eat and drink appropriately, which can be detrimental to their health and
well-being.
The male black bear (Samson, 4yr) was observed for approximately 20 minutes pacing in front of the fence that is located
under a walking bridge that is directly across from the maintenance gate of the cougar enclosure. According to facility
representatives, the cougar directly across from where the bear was pacing was vocalizing and was in heat, and could
have been prompting the bear’s sudden pacing. The cougar’s fence and the bear’s fence are approximately 30-40ft apart.
There was an approximately 12ft- to 15ft-long pathway created by the male’s pacing over time. The male did stop pacing
as the inspection carried on up the hill. The male came to bathe and interact, then later went back down near the fence
and started pacing again. The behavior had only previously been observed by the facility at feeding times.
Development of pacing in bears can be a sign of pain and/or distress.
The lack of daily observations of possible health issues or behavior changes in a timely manner can cause a minor
treatable condition to become chronic or serious, causing unnecessary pain or discomfort the the regulated animal.
The licensee must ensure that daily observation of all animals occurs to assess their health and well-being; additionally, a
mechanism of direct and frequent communication is required so that timely and accurate information on problems of
animal health, behavior, and well-being is conveyed to the attending veterinarian.

There is a cement bathing area (approx. 6ftx6ft x3ft) in the bear enclosure that has a buildup of dark green algae around
the walls of the bath, and the water was brown in color. The bears have continual access to fresh, potable water to drink
in the lockdown area of the enclosure.
Unclean water receptacles can be a possible source of disease or other health issues if the animals drink and/or bathe in
the water.
The cement bathing area must be cleaned and maintained to prevent the buildup of algae/dirt/debris. All water
receptacles shall be kept clean and sanitary.

The pond (approx. 6ft x 4ft x 0.5ft) located in the bald eagle enclosure had a buildup of algae along the sides of the pond.
There was a large accumulation of plant material in the upper pond section. Facility representatives said the pond pump
was not working and had not been replaced yet. The facility’s staff had not undertaken regular maintenance of the pond
for approximately 2 to 3 months, allowing time for the buildup of algae growth in the water, according to facility
representatives.
The lack of proper cleaning and maintenance of a pond that birds have access to for bathing or feeding can be a possible
source of health issues due to algae and/or bacterial growth.
Pools or other aquatic areas with drainage systems must provide adequate drainage and must be located so that all of the
water contained in such pools or other aquatic areas may be effectively eliminated when necessary for cleaning the pool
or other aquatic area or for other purposes.
This was a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class C license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the
applicant.
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspections or by July 18, 2026 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection.
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
There were three domestic pigeons (2-Blue bar, 1-Blue/White) that had overgrown upper beaks that protruded downward 
past the lower beak. The overgrown beaks had not been identified by facility representatives.  
 
Overgrown beaks can impact a bird's ability to eat and drink appropriately, which can be detrimental to their health and 
well-being.  
 
The male black bear (Samson, 4yr) was observed for approximately 20 minutes pacing in front of the fence that is located 
under a walking bridge that is directly across from the maintenance gate of the cougar enclosure. According to facility

and started pacing again. The behavior had only previously been observed by the facility at feeding times.

The lack of daily observations of possible health issues or behavior changes in a timely manner can cause a minor 
treatable condition to become chronic or serious, causing unnecessary pain or discomfort the the regulated animal.  
 
The licensee must ensure that daily observation of all animals occurs to assess their health and well-being; additionally, a 
mechanism of direct and frequent communication is required so that timely and accurate information on problems of 
animal health, behavior, and well-being is conveyed to the attending veterinarian. 
3.130 
   
Watering. 
There is a cement bathing area (approx. 6ftx6ft x3ft) in the bear enclosure that has a buildup of dark green algae around 
the walls of the bath, and the water was brown in color. The bears have continual access to fresh, potable water to drink

the water. 
 
The cement bathing area must be cleaned and maintained to prevent the buildup of algae/dirt/debris. All water 
receptacles shall be kept clean and sanitary. 
3.157(a)(2) 
   
Water quality. 
The pond (approx. 6ft x 4ft x 0.5ft) located in the bald eagle enclosure had a buildup of algae along the sides of the pond. 
There was a large accumulation of plant material in the upper pond section. Facility representatives said the pond pump 
was not working and had not been replaced yet. The facility’s staff had not undertaken regular maintenance of the pond

The lack of proper cleaning and maintenance of a pond that birds have access to for bathing or feeding can be a possible 
source of health issues due to algae and/or bacterial growth. 
 
Pools or other aquatic areas with drainage systems must provide adequate drainage and must be located so that all of the 
water contained in such pools or other aquatic areas may be effectively eliminated when necessary for cleaning the pool 
or other aquatic area or for other purposes. 
 
This was a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class C license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the 
applicant.  
 
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspections or by July 18, 2026 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee 
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection. 
 
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires.
